title: Uterine Fibroids
oneLiner: Benign uterine smooth muscle tumours, common in reproductive-age women, causing heavy menstrual bleeding, pelvic pain, and infertility.

sections.Killers First — What Will Kill Them In T:
- **Acute massive haemorrhage:** Hypotension, tachycardia, pallor, soaking pads per hour — treat as shock, not just fibroids. **Single finding:** SBP <90 or HR >110 with heavy vaginal bleeding.
- **Torsion of pedunculated fibroid:** Sudden severe pelvic pain, vomiting, peritonism — mimics ovarian torsion, ectopic, or appendicitis. **Single finding:** Acute unilateral pain with a tender mobile mass on bimanual exam.
- **Septic degeneration (rare):** Fever, foul discharge, hypotension — suspect if recent instrumentation or necrosis. **Single finding:** Temp >38.5°C with diffuse uterine tenderness and rising lactate.
sections.Presentation:
- **Cardinal symptoms:**
  - Heavy menstrual bleeding (menorrhagia) — soaking pads, clots, flooding
  - Pelvic pressure, bloating, urinary frequency, constipation
  - Chronic pelvic pain or dysmenorrhea
  - Infertility or recurrent miscarriage
- **Key exam findings:**
  - Palpable irregular bulky uterus on abdominal exam
  - On bimanual: enlarged, firm, nodular uterus; may ballot well if pedunculated
  - Blood on speculum if actively bleeding; cervix usually closed
sections.Differential Diagnosis:
- **Adenomyosis** — diffusely tender, boggy uterus on exam; MRI shows junctional zone thickening
- **Ovarian mass** — separate from uterus on bimanual; ultrasound shows adnexal origin
- **Endometrial polyp** — focal lesion on ultrasound; hysteroscopy for diagnosis
- **Endometriosis** — cyclical pain, tender nodules on uterosacral ligaments; imaging may be normal
- **Pregnancy-related (e.g., miscarriage, ectopic)** — positive β-hCG; always test
- **Pelvic inflammatory disease** — bilateral tenderness, cervical motion tenderness, discharge, fever
- **Leiomyosarcoma (rare)** — rapid growth, postmenopausal, irregular borders on MRI; cannot be ruled out on imaging alone
- **Splitter:** Transvaginal ultrasound (TVUS) — well-defined hypoechoic mass within myometrium = fibroid; diffuse myometrial heterogeneity + tenderness = adenomyosis
sections.Investigations:
- **Bedside:**
  - Urine pregnancy test (β-hCG) — **mandatory** in any reproductive-age woman with bleeding/pain
  - Vital signs — assess for shock
  - Speculum exam + bimanual
- **Bloods:**
  - FBC (for anaemia, Hb <100? check local policy for transfusion threshold) — [check local policy]
  - Iron studies (ferritin, TIBC) — confirm iron deficiency
  - Coagulation screen (PT/APTT) if very heavy menses — rule out bleeding disorder
  - LDH (if suspect sarcoma – elevated)
- **Imaging:**
  - **First-line:** Transvaginal ultrasound (TVUS) with Doppler — number, size, location (submucosal, intramural, subserosal, pedunculated) plus endometrial thickness
  - **Second-line:** MRI pelvis with contrast — best for mapping before surgery/embolisation, risk stratification for sarcoma (high ADC, central necrosis)
- **Special tests:**
  - Hysteroscopy with biopsy if submucosal fibroid or endometrial pathology suspected
  - Endometrial biopsy if postmenopausal bleeding
- **Diagnostic criteria / scoring system:**
  - **FIGO classification** (Palmer system) for fibroid location (0-8) – used for surgical planning
  - **Pictorial Blood Loss Assessment Chart (PBAC)** – score >100 suggests menorrhagia
- **Never-miss test:** Urine β-hCG – omission can lead to missed ectopic or miscarriage, wrong management.
sections.Management:
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - If haemodynamically unstable (SBP <90, HR >110): **resuscitate per shock protocol** – wide-bore IV, crystalloid bolus 500-1000mL, crossmatch, consider tranexamic acid 1g IV (contraindicated if active thrombosis).
  - If stable but heavy bleeding: tranexamic acid 1g IV/PO, mefenamic acid 500mg PO.
  - Analgesia: paracetamol + NSAID (e.g., ibuprofen 400mg PO) if no contraindications.
  - Arrange admission for IV iron or transfusion if Hb <70 or symptomatic anaemia – [check local policy].
- **Ward (first 24-72h):**
  - Monitor bleeding (pad count), observations (4-hourly), urine output.
  - Iron replacement: IV ferric carboxymaltose (Ferinject) 15mg/kg up to 1000mg or IV iron sucrose (Venofer) – follow local protocol.
  - Transfuse to Hb >80 if symptomatic (palpitations, dyspnoea, chest pain) – [check local policy].
  - Start symptomatic treatment: tranexamic acid 1g TDS during menses (PO) + mefenamic acid 500mg TDS PRN (max 5 days).
  - Consider initiating Mirena IUS (levonorgestrel-releasing IUD) for continuous progestin effect – discuss with gynaecologist; can be inserted on ward if no contraindications.
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - Medical: COCP (e.g., Rigevidon) cyclical or continuous; Mirena IUS; GnRH agonists (goserelin 3.6mg SC monthly) for pre-operative shrinkage or symptom control (max 6 months due to bone loss).
  - Surgical: myomectomy (hysteroscopic, laparoscopic, open), uterine artery embolisation, MRI-guided focused ultrasound, hysterectomy.
  - Refer to gynaecology clinic within 4-6 weeks for shared decision-making.
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**
  - **IV fluids:** 0.9% saline or Hartmann's at [check local policy] mL/h only if nil by mouth or hypotensive; otherwise oral.
  - **VTE prophylaxis:** Enoxaparin 40mg SC daily (if CrCl >30, no active bleeding) – start after haemodynamic stability confirmed.
  - **Diet:** Normal if tolerating; NBM if planned surgery.
  - **Obs frequency:** 4-hourly (HR, BP, RR, temperature, NEWS2); more frequent if unstable.
sections.Drugs & Doses:
- **First-line agents:**
  - **Tranexamic acid** (Cyklokapron) — 1g IV/PO TDS during menses (max 5 days) — reduces bleeding by ~30%.
  - **Mefenamic acid** (Ponstan) — 500mg TDS PO with or after food (max 5 days) — also reduces prostaglandins.
  - **Mirena IUS** (levonorgestrel 20μg/24h) — inserted for 5 years; first-line for heavy bleeding.
- **Common alternatives:**
  - **COCP** (ethinylestradiol 20-35μg + progestin) — cyclical or tricycle.
  - **Norethisterone** (Primolut N) — 5mg TDS PO days 5-26 of cycle (or continuous) – used if COCP contraindicated.
  - **GnRH agonist:** Goserelin (Zoladex) 3.6mg SC monthly ± add-back HRT (e.g., tibolone) if >6 months required.
- **Key contraindications / interactions:**
  - Tranexamic acid: active thromboembolism, DVT/PE history, CV disease, colour blindness.
  - Mefenamic acid: asthma, peptic ulcer disease, renal impairment (avoid if eGFR <30).
  - COCP: migraines with aura, history of thromboembolism, liver disease, >35y smoker.
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:**
  - Mefenamic acid: avoid in severe renal impairment (CrCl <30) – reduces renal excretion.
  - Tranexamic acid: dose reduction in renal impairment (CrCl 30-50: 50% dose; CrCl <30: contraindicated) – [check local policy].
- **Most dangerous dose error:**
  - **Tranexamic acid given without checking VTE risk** – can precipitate thrombosis. Always document VTE risk assessment before prescribing.
sections.Complications:
- **Acute:**
  - Hypovolaemic shock from acute haemorrhage
  - Torsion of pedunculated fibroid → ovarian necrosis
  - Red degeneration (pain, low-grade fever, nausea) — self-limiting, managed with supportive care (analgesia, rest)
  - Infection (rare) — fever, discharge, sepsis; treat with broad-spectrum antibiotics (e.g., co-amoxiclav 625mg TDS)
- **Chronic:**
  - Iron deficiency anaemia (IDA) — fatigue, pallor, brittle nails, pica
  - Infertility and pregnancy complications (miscarriage, preterm labour, malpresentation)
  - Obstructive symptoms — hydronephrosis, renal impairment (large fibroids)
  - Malignant transformation: **leiomyosarcoma** – extremely rare (<0.5%), consider if rapid growth, postmenopausal, irregular borders, elevated LDH.
sections.Prognosis: - Benign disease; most fibroids regress after menopause. Symptom severity correlates with size and location. Heavy bleeding can cause disabling anaemia; with medical/surgical treatment, quality of life improves. Malignant risk is low but cannot be excluded on imaging alone.
sections.Pearls (positive actions):
- **Always check a pregnancy test** in reproductive-age women with abdominal pain or bleeding – a positive result changes everything.
- **Offer Mirena IUS as first-line** for heavy menstrual bleeding with fibroids unless they desire pregnancy soon – it's highly effective, long-acting, and low systemic side effects.
- **Measure LDH and lactate** in any rapidly growing fibroid (especially in perimenopausal or postmenopausal women) to raise suspicion for sarcoma.
sections.Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid):
- **Do not assume heavy bleeding is only fibroids** – always rule out coagulopathy, endometrial pathology, and pregnancy.
- **Do not start COCP without assessing VTE risk** – check personal/family history, immobility, >35y smoking.
- **Do not prescribe tranexamic acid without VTE risk assessment** – can cause serious thromboembolism in at-risk patients.
- **Do not dismiss pain as "just degeneration"** – always consider torsion (urgent ultrasound with colour Doppler; if avascular – call gynaecology immediately).
sections.Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home):
- **Return if:**
  - Soaking >1 pad per hour for >2 hours
  - Passing clots larger than a golf ball
  - Severe, worsening pelvic pain not relieved by oral analgesia
  - Fever, chills, or foul-smelling discharge
- **Follow-up:** Gynaecology outpatient clinic in 4-6 weeks; or sooner if symptoms not controlled with first-line medical therapy.
sections.Escalation Criteria:
- **Call senior (reg/consultant) if:**
  - Haemodynamically unstable despite initial resuscitation (SBP <90, HR >110, lactate >2)
  - Hb <70 with active bleeding or symptomatic anaemia requiring blood transfusion
  - NEWS2 ≥5 or rising
  - Severe pain with peritonism / suspected torsion
  - Rapidly growing fibroid (especially if postmenopausal) – potential sarcoma
- **Call ICU/crash team if:**
  - Ongoing haemorrhage requiring >2 units of blood per hour
  - GCS <15 or seizure
  - Lactate >4 mmol/L refractory to fluids
  - Signs of impending shock (low urine output <0.5 mL/kg/h, increasing vasopressor requirements)

rawMarkdown:
## Uterine Fibroids
**One-liner:** Benign uterine smooth muscle tumours, common in reproductive-age women, causing heavy menstrual bleeding, pelvic pain, and infertility.

### Killers First — What Will Kill Them In The First Hour?
- **Acute massive haemorrhage:** Hypotension, tachycardia, pallor, soaking pads per hour — treat as shock, not just fibroids. **Single finding:** SBP <90 or HR >110 with heavy vaginal bleeding.
- **Torsion of pedunculated fibroid:** Sudden severe pelvic pain, vomiting, peritonism — mimics ovarian torsion, ectopic, or appendicitis. **Single finding:** Acute unilateral pain with a tender mobile mass on bimanual exam.
- **Septic degeneration (rare):** Fever, foul discharge, hypotension — suspect if recent instrumentation or necrosis. **Single finding:** Temp >38.5°C with diffuse uterine tenderness and rising lactate.

### Presentation
- **Cardinal symptoms:**
  - Heavy menstrual bleeding (menorrhagia) — soaking pads, clots, flooding
  - Pelvic pressure, bloating, urinary frequency, constipation
  - Chronic pelvic pain or dysmenorrhea
  - Infertility or recurrent miscarriage
- **Key exam findings:**
  - Palpable irregular bulky uterus on abdominal exam
  - On bimanual: enlarged, firm, nodular uterus; may ballot well if pedunculated
  - Blood on speculum if actively bleeding; cervix usually closed

### Differential Diagnosis
- **Adenomyosis** — diffusely tender, boggy uterus on exam; MRI shows junctional zone thickening
- **Ovarian mass** — separate from uterus on bimanual; ultrasound shows adnexal origin
- **Endometrial polyp** — focal lesion on ultrasound; hysteroscopy for diagnosis
- **Endometriosis** — cyclical pain, tender nodules on uterosacral ligaments; imaging may be normal
- **Pregnancy-related (e.g., miscarriage, ectopic)** — positive β-hCG; always test
- **Pelvic inflammatory disease** — bilateral tenderness, cervical motion tenderness, discharge, fever
- **Leiomyosarcoma (rare)** — rapid growth, postmenopausal, irregular borders on MRI; cannot be ruled out on imaging alone
- **Splitter:** Transvaginal ultrasound (TVUS) — well-defined hypoechoic mass within myometrium = fibroid; diffuse myometrial heterogeneity + tenderness = adenomyosis

### Investigations
- **Bedside:**
  - Urine pregnancy test (β-hCG) — **mandatory** in any reproductive-age woman with bleeding/pain
  - Vital signs — assess for shock
  - Speculum exam + bimanual
- **Bloods:**
  - FBC (for anaemia, Hb <100? check local policy for transfusion threshold) — [check local policy]
  - Iron studies (ferritin, TIBC) — confirm iron deficiency
  - Coagulation screen (PT/APTT) if very heavy menses — rule out bleeding disorder
  - LDH (if suspect sarcoma – elevated)
- **Imaging:**
  - **First-line:** Transvaginal ultrasound (TVUS) with Doppler — number, size, location (submucosal, intramural, subserosal, pedunculated) plus endometrial thickness
  - **Second-line:** MRI pelvis with contrast — best for mapping before surgery/embolisation, risk stratification for sarcoma (high ADC, central necrosis)
- **Special tests:**
  - Hysteroscopy with biopsy if submucosal fibroid or endometrial pathology suspected
  - Endometrial biopsy if postmenopausal bleeding
- **Diagnostic criteria / scoring system:**
  - **FIGO classification** (Palmer system) for fibroid location (0-8) – used for surgical planning
  - **Pictorial Blood Loss Assessment Chart (PBAC)** – score >100 suggests menorrhagia
- **Never-miss test:** Urine β-hCG – omission can lead to missed ectopic or miscarriage, wrong management.

### Management
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - If haemodynamically unstable (SBP <90, HR >110): **resuscitate per shock protocol** – wide-bore IV, crystalloid bolus 500-1000mL, crossmatch, consider tranexamic acid 1g IV (contraindicated if active thrombosis).
  - If stable but heavy bleeding: tranexamic acid 1g IV/PO, mefenamic acid 500mg PO.
  - Analgesia: paracetamol + NSAID (e.g., ibuprofen 400mg PO) if no contraindications.
  - Arrange admission for IV iron or transfusion if Hb <70 or symptomatic anaemia – [check local policy].
- **Ward (first 24-72h):**
  - Monitor bleeding (pad count), observations (4-hourly), urine output.
  - Iron replacement: IV ferric carboxymaltose (Ferinject) 15mg/kg up to 1000mg or IV iron sucrose (Venofer) – follow local protocol.
  - Transfuse to Hb >80 if symptomatic (palpitations, dyspnoea, chest pain) – [check local policy].
  - Start symptomatic treatment: tranexamic acid 1g TDS during menses (PO) + mefenamic acid 500mg TDS PRN (max 5 days).
  - Consider initiating Mirena IUS (levonorgestrel-releasing IUD) for continuous progestin effect – discuss with gynaecologist; can be inserted on ward if no contraindications.
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - Medical: COCP (e.g., Rigevidon) cyclical or continuous; Mirena IUS; GnRH agonists (goserelin 3.6mg SC monthly) for pre-operative shrinkage or symptom control (max 6 months due to bone loss).
  - Surgical: myomectomy (hysteroscopic, laparoscopic, open), uterine artery embolisation, MRI-guided focused ultrasound, hysterectomy.
  - Refer to gynaecology clinic within 4-6 weeks for shared decision-making.
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**
  - **IV fluids:** 0.9% saline or Hartmann's at [check local policy] mL/h only if nil by mouth or hypotensive; otherwise oral.
  - **VTE prophylaxis:** Enoxaparin 40mg SC daily (if CrCl >30, no active bleeding) – start after haemodynamic stability confirmed.
  - **Diet:** Normal if tolerating; NBM if planned surgery.
  - **Obs frequency:** 4-hourly (HR, BP, RR, temperature, NEWS2); more frequent if unstable.

### Drugs & Doses
- **First-line agents:**
  - **Tranexamic acid** (Cyklokapron) — 1g IV/PO TDS during menses (max 5 days) — reduces bleeding by ~30%.
  - **Mefenamic acid** (Ponstan) — 500mg TDS PO with or after food (max 5 days) — also reduces prostaglandins.
  - **Mirena IUS** (levonorgestrel 20μg/24h) — inserted for 5 years; first-line for heavy bleeding.
- **Common alternatives:**
  - **COCP** (ethinylestradiol 20-35μg + progestin) — cyclical or tricycle.
  - **Norethisterone** (Primolut N) — 5mg TDS PO days 5-26 of cycle (or continuous) – used if COCP contraindicated.
  - **GnRH agonist:** Goserelin (Zoladex) 3.6mg SC monthly ± add-back HRT (e.g., tibolone) if >6 months required.
- **Key contraindications / interactions:**
  - Tranexamic acid: active thromboembolism, DVT/PE history, CV disease, colour blindness.
  - Mefenamic acid: asthma, peptic ulcer disease, renal impairment (avoid if eGFR <30).
  - COCP: migraines with aura, history of thromboembolism, liver disease, >35y smoker.
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:**
  - Mefenamic acid: avoid in severe renal impairment (CrCl <30) – reduces renal excretion.
  - Tranexamic acid: dose reduction in renal impairment (CrCl 30-50: 50% dose; CrCl <30: contraindicated) – [check local policy].
- **Most dangerous dose error:**
  - **Tranexamic acid given without checking VTE risk** – can precipitate thrombosis. Always document VTE risk assessment before prescribing.

### Complications
- **Acute:**
  - Hypovolaemic shock from acute haemorrhage
  - Torsion of pedunculated fibroid → ovarian necrosis
  - Red degeneration (pain, low-grade fever, nausea) — self-limiting, managed with supportive care (analgesia, rest)
  - Infection (rare) — fever, discharge, sepsis; treat with broad-spectrum antibiotics (e.g., co-amoxiclav 625mg TDS)
- **Chronic:**
  - Iron deficiency anaemia (IDA) — fatigue, pallor, brittle nails, pica
  - Infertility and pregnancy complications (miscarriage, preterm labour, malpresentation)
  - Obstructive symptoms — hydronephrosis, renal impairment (large fibroids)
  - Malignant transformation: **leiomyosarcoma** – extremely rare (<0.5%), consider if rapid growth, postmenopausal, irregular borders, elevated LDH.

### Prognosis
- Benign disease; most fibroids regress after menopause. Symptom severity correlates with size and location. Heavy bleeding can cause disabling anaemia; with medical/surgical treatment, quality of life improves. Malignant risk is low but cannot be excluded on imaging alone.

### Pearls (positive actions)
- **Always check a pregnancy test** in reproductive-age women with abdominal pain or bleeding – a positive result changes everything.
- **Offer Mirena IUS as first-line** for heavy menstrual bleeding with fibroids unless they desire pregnancy soon – it's highly effective, long-acting, and low systemic side effects.
- **Measure LDH and lactate** in any rapidly growing fibroid (especially in perimenopausal or postmenopausal women) to raise suspicion for sarcoma.

### Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid)
- **Do not assume heavy bleeding is only fibroids** – always rule out coagulopathy, endometrial pathology, and pregnancy.
- **Do not start COCP without assessing VTE risk** – check personal/family history, immobility, >35y smoking.
- **Do not prescribe tranexamic acid without VTE risk assessment** – can cause serious thromboembolism in at-risk patients.
- **Do not dismiss pain as "just degeneration"** – always consider torsion (urgent ultrasound with colour Doppler; if avascular – call gynaecology immediately).

### Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home)
- **Return if:**
  - Soaking >1 pad per hour for >2 hours
  - Passing clots larger than a golf ball
  - Severe, worsening pelvic pain not relieved by oral analgesia
  - Fever, chills, or foul-smelling discharge
- **Follow-up:** Gynaecology outpatient clinic in 4-6 weeks; or sooner if symptoms not controlled with first-line medical therapy.

### Escalation Criteria
- **Call senior (reg/consultant) if:**
  - Haemodynamically unstable despite initial resuscitation (SBP <90, HR >110, lactate >2)
  - Hb <70 with active bleeding or symptomatic anaemia requiring blood transfusion
  - NEWS2 ≥5 or rising
  - Severe pain with peritonism / suspected torsion
  - Rapidly growing fibroid (especially if postmenopausal) – potential sarcoma
- **Call ICU/crash team if:**
  - Ongoing haemorrhage requiring >2 units of blood per hour
  - GCS <15 or seizure
  - Lactate >4 mmol/L refractory to fluids
  - Signs of impending shock (low urine output <0.5 mL/kg/h, increasing vasopressor requirements)
